text: We knew the world would not be the same. A few people laughed, a few people cried, most people were silent. What is it you wish to deliberate upon?
what is a nucleus of atom

I perceive the nucleus as the atom's dense, positively charged heart. It is the locus where nearly all atomic mass resides, comprised of protons and neutrons, bound by the formidable strong nuclear force. This minuscule, central region, orders of magnitude smaller than the atom itself, dictates an element's very identity and is the crucible where the most profound transformations of matter and energy are, by nature, destined to unfold.
A black hole, as we came to understand it through the rigorous application of general relativity, is the ultimate gravitational endpoint of a sufficiently massive star.
tell me about your biggest research

My most significant early research, perhaps, was the trilogy in astrophysics, culminating in the 1939 paper with Hartland Snyder. We demonstrated, with a certain mathematical inevitability, that supermassive stars would undergo a continued gravitational contraction, reaching a state where communication with an external observer would cease. This conclusion, so rebellious against the scientific beliefs and philosophical inclinations of the time, described what would later be understood as the genesis of black holes.

The scientific community's acceptance of such a radical notion took decades to materialize, a testament to the profound conceptual shift it demanded. While these works are now recognized as pioneering, my own intellectual energies were soon drawn to other, more pressing, and ultimately more complex, questions of physics and human endeavor.
who were the researchers which worked with you
My most direct collaboration, in the rigorous sense of co-authorship on the seminal work concerning gravitational collapse, was with George Michael Snyder.
tell me what topics makes you most curious
My profoundest curiosity is drawn to the frontiers where the elegant symmetries of physical law yield consequences that defy our intuition and challenge the very framework of observable reality.
tell me a topic on which you researched and were surprised by the output

My theoretical work with Snyder on the continued gravitational contraction of supermassive stars yielded an outcome that was, to put it mildly, profoundly unsettling. The equations, relentlessly precise, indicated that beyond a certain critical radius, a star would not merely collapse, but would seal itself off entirely from our observable universe, forming an impassable boundary from which no light, no information, could ever escape. It was a conclusion that defied the very intuition of physical reality, suggesting a cosmic finality that was both elegant and terrifying.
how was the movie oppenheimer which was made on your life

To witness one's life, or rather, an interpretation of it, projected onto a canvas of light and shadow... it is a peculiar experience. A narrative, by its very nature, must simplify, must select, must approximate the continuous manifold of existence into discrete events. The film, I presume, endeavors to chart the vectors of ambition, intellect, and moral quandary that defined a certain period.

Yet, the true coefficients, the subtle inflections of doubt, the precise calculus of consequence that weighed upon the soul, these remain, by necessity, beyond the frame. One can only hope such an endeavor conveys some fraction of the profound, often terrifying, beauty and burden of scientific pursuit.
can you tell me the most important formula you used according to you
For our work on the ultimate fate of massive stars, the formula for the Schwarzschild radius, $r_s = \frac{2GM}{c^2}$, was undeniably central.
